title: The Master Masons of Gothic Architecture
excerpt: From the 11th century, extraordinary men of vision — bishops, architects, and master masons — created buildings that were displays of temporal power and artistic achievement.
body_html:
From the 11th century, architectural activity took centre stage in the building of expanding towns and cities. The king continued to play the role of the leading patron, however this function was also taken up by the clergy, bishops, abbots, laity, communities and associations.

Visionaries and Their Patrons

The initiation of large construction projects was linked to the presence of extraordinary men of vision — some were bishops such as Fulbert of Chartres at the beginning of the 11th century or Maurice de Sully at Paris in 1160. By the second half of the 11th century, the architect established himself as the one who would draw up the project, design it and carry its execution.

The monks of the Canterbury Cathedral called in several architects, some English, others from Île-de-France. They chose William of Sens who elaborated on the parts to be reconstructed and the ones that could be preserved. This is how the first fully Gothic building came to be constructed on English soil.

Architects Immortalised in Stone

The architects were represented on tombstones drawing complicated designs. In Notre Dame in Paris, Jean de Chelles' name has been inscribed in Gothic lettering as he had laid the first stone of the south transept in 1258.

In the late 13th century, architects' names were inscribed in the labyrinths laid out on the nave floor. At Reims Cathedral, the Archbishop Aubry de Humbert appears in the centre of the labyrinth surrounded by master masons: Jean d'Orbais, Jean Le Loup, Gaucher de Reims and Bernard de Soissons.

Pierre de Montreuil was honoured with the university title of 'Doctor Lathomorum' (doctor of stones) and is entombed with his wife in the Lady Chapel which he had built — exemplifying the deep relationship between patron and architect that drove the creation of Gothic masterpieces.

Teams of Master Craftsmen

Architects required a high calibre of craftsmen: stone dressers, stone cutters, apprentices, carriers, mortar mixers, metal workers, and blacksmiths. Étienne de Bonneuil recruited his team of expert technicians in Paris to build the Uppsala Cathedral in Sweden in 1287. William Wynford, one of the most successful English master masons of the 14th century, worked on Wells Cathedral, Windsor Castle, New College at Oxford, and Winchester College.
